questions.question: Explain the difference between flow control and congestion control in TCP.
keyPoints: Flow control prevents sender from overwhelming receiver (end-to-end)
Congestion control prevents network overload (router/switch level)
Flow control uses receive window (rwnd)
Congestion control uses congestion window (cwnd)
Both work together to ensure efficient data transmission

questions.question: Describe how TCP implements flow control using the sliding window protocol.
keyPoints: TCP uses a window of unacknowledged packets
Receiver advertises available buffer space via Window Size field
Sender can send up to window size bytes without waiting for ACK
ACKs slide the window forward, allowing new data transmission
Zero window condition pauses transmission until buffer frees up

questions.question: Why is UDP preferred for real-time applications like VoIP and video streaming?
keyPoints: UDP has lower overhead (no connection setup)
No retransmission delays that disrupt timing
Minimal processing at sender/receiver ends
Real-time data can tolerate some loss but not latency
Header size smaller than TCP (8 bytes vs 20 bytes)

questions.question: Explain the three-way handshake process in TCP connection establishment.
keyPoints: Step 1: Client sends SYN segment with initial sequence number
Step 2: Server responds with SYN-ACK (acknowledges SYN, sends its initial sequence number)
Step 3: Client sends ACK to confirm
After three-way handshake, connection is established and reliable data transfer begins

questions.question: Compare Stop-and-Wait ARQ and Selective Repeat ARQ protocols.
keyPoints: Stop-and-Wait: sends one packet, waits for ACK, inefficient for high latency
Selective Repeat: sends multiple packets, only retransmits lost ones, more efficient
Stop-and-Wait requires simple logic but low throughput
Selective Repeat needs buffer at receiver to reorder packets
Selective Repeat better for networks with high packet loss or latency

questions.question: What happens when a TCP receiver's buffer becomes full? Explain the zero window mechanism.
keyPoints: Receiver advertises Window Size = 0 in TCP header
Sender stops transmitting data upon receiving zero window
Sender sends periodic probe segments to check window size
When receiver has buffer space, it advertises non-zero window
This prevents data overflow at receiver while maintaining connection

questions.question: Explain the working of Go-Back-N ARQ protocol with an example.
keyPoints: Sender can transmit N packets without waiting for ACK
Receiver discards all packets after a lost packet
Sender retransmits all packets from the lost one onwards
Example: If sending 1,2,3,4,5 and packet 3 is lost, receiver discards 4,5
Sender times out waiting for ACK of 3, retransmits 3,4,5

questions.question: Explain the TCP header structure and identify the fields used for flow control. How do these fields work together to implement reliable data transfer?
keyPoints: TCP Header: 20-60 bytes with source/destination ports, sequence/ACK numbers, data offset, flags, window size, checksum, urgent pointer, options
Window Size field: Primary flow control mechanism - advertises receive buffer capacity
Sequence Number: Ensures ordered delivery and identifies data bytes
ACK Number: Confirms received data, helps sender know what to retransmit
Flags (SYN, ACK, FIN, RST, PSH, URG): Control connection states and data handling
Checksum: Error detection for data integrity
Working together: Sequence numbers track data order, ACKs confirm receipt, Window Size regulates sender rate, Flags manage connection lifecycle

questions.question: Describe in detail the mechanisms used by TCP to ensure reliable data transmission. How does it differ from UDP in handling reliability?
keyPoints: TCP Reliability Mechanisms: Error detection via checksum, sequencing via sequence numbers, acknowledgments (ACK/NAK), retransmission via timers, flow control via sliding window, congestion control
Sequence numbers ensure ordered delivery and help identify missing segments
ACKs confirm successful receipt; cumulative ACKs indicate next expected byte
Retransmission triggered by timeout or duplicate ACKs
Flow control prevents receiver overflow using advertised window
UDP: Only provides checksum for error detection, no sequencing, no ACKs, no retransmission, no flow control
UDP sacrifices reliability for speed, suitable for real-time applications

questions.question: Explain the concept of sliding window protocol in TCP. Discuss the advantages of using a sliding window over stop-and-wait protocol.
keyPoints: Sliding window allows multiple packets (equal to window size) to be in transit
Window has three regions: sent & acknowledged, sent but unacknowledged, not yet sent
Sender maintains variables: Last ACK Received (LAR), Last Segment Sent (LSS)
Advantages over Stop-and-Wait: Higher throughput, better utilization of bandwidth, handles propagation delay efficiently, pipelined transmission
Window size can be dynamically adjusted based on network conditions
Disadvantage: More complex implementation, requires buffer management at sender and receiver
